The Gooſe was cut i' the head, to heare it too:
Brave Chant-it-cleare, his noble heart was done;
His combe was cut: And two or three o' his wives,
Or faireſt Concubines, had their necks broke,
Ere they would zee this day: To marke the verven
Heart of a beaſt, the very Pig, the Pig,
This very mornin, as hee was a roſting
Cry'd out his eyes, and made a ſhow as hee would
Ha' bit in two the ſpit, as he would ſay;
There ſhall no roſt-meat be this diſmall day.
And zure, I thinke, If I had not got his tongue
Betweene my teeth, and eate it, he had ſpoke it.
Well, I will in, and cry too; never leave
Crying, untill our maids may drive a Buck
With my ſalt teares at the next waſhing day.

sp.speaker: Pre.
sp.l: KEepe out thoſe fellowes; Ile ha' none come in,
But the High Conſtable, the man of peace,
And the Queenes Captaine, the brave man of warre.
Now neighbour Turfe, the cauſe why you are call'd,
Before me by my warrant, but unſpecified,
Is this; and pray you marke it thoroughly!
Here is a Gentleman, and as it ſeemes,
Both of good birth, faire ſpeech, and peaceable,
Who was this morning rob'd here in the wood:
You for your part a man of good report,
Of credit, landed, and of faire demeanes,
And by authority, high Conſtable;
Are notwithſtanding touch'd in this complaint,
Of being careleſſe in the Huy and Cry.
I cannot chooſe but grieve a Soldiers loſſe:
And I am ſory too for your neglect,
Being my neighbour; this is all I object.

sp.speaker: Hug.
sp.l: This is not all; I can alledge far more,
And almoſt urge him for an acceſſorie.
Good Mr Juſtice gi'me leave to ſpeake,
For I am Plaintife. Let not neighbour-hood
Make him ſecure, or ſtand on priviledge.

sp.speaker: Pre.
sp.l: Sir, I dare uſe no partiality:
Object then what you pleaſe, ſo it be truth.

sp.speaker: Hug.
sp.l: This more: and which is more, then he can anſwer,
Beſide his letting fall the Huy, and Cry
He doth protect the man, charg'd with the felonie,
And keepes him hid I heare, within his houſe,
Becauſe he is affied unto his Daughter.

sp.speaker: Tur.
sp.l: I doe defie 'hun, ſo ſhall ſhee doe too.
I pray your worſhips favour, le' me have hearing.
I doe conveſſe, 'twas told me ſuch a velonie,
And't not diſgriev'd me a little when 'twas told me,
Vor I was going to Church, to marry Awdrey:
And who ſhould marry her, but this very Clay,
Who was charg'd to be the chiefe theife o' hun all.
Now I (the halter ſtick me, if I tell,
Your worſhips any leazins did fore-thinke 'un
The trueſt man, till he waz run away.
I thought, I had had 'un as zure as in a zaw-pit,
Or i' mine Oven. Nay, i' the Towne-pound.
I was za ſure o' hun: I'ld ha' gi'n my life for 'un,
Till he did ſtart. But now, I zee 'un guilty,
Az var as I can looke at 'un. Would you ha' more?

sp.speaker: Hug.
sp.l: Yes, I will have Sir what the Law will give me.
You gave your word to ſee him ſafe, forth comming;
I challenge that: But, that is forfeited;
Beſide, your careleſneſſe in the purſuit,
Argues your ſlackneſſe, and neglect of dutie,
Which ought be puniſh'd with ſeverity.

sp.speaker: Pre.
sp.l: He ſpeakes but reaſon

sp.speaker: Turfe.
sp.l: Bring forth the man,
And you are quit: But otherwiſe, your word
Binds you to make amends for all his loſſe,
And thinke your ſelfe befriended, if he take it
Without a farder ſuit, or going to law.
Come to a compoſition with him, Turfe:
The Law is coſtly, and will draw on charge.

sp.speaker: Tur.
sp.l: Yes, I doe know, I vurſt mun vee a Returney,
And then make legges to my great man o' Law,
To be o' my counſell, and take trouble-vees,
And yet zay nothing vor me, but deviſe
All diſtrict meanes, to ranſackle me o' my money.
A Peſt'lence prick the throats o' hun. I doe know hun
As well az I waz i' their bellies, and brought up there.
What would you ha' me doe? what would you aske of me?

sp.speaker: Hug.
sp.l: I aske the reſtitution of my money;
And will not bate one penny o' the ſumme:
Foure ſcore, and five pound. I aske, beſides,
Amendment for my hurts; my paine, and ſuffering
Are loſſe enough for me, Sir, to ſit downe with,
Ile put it to your worſhip; what you award me,
Ile take; and gi' him a generall releaſe.

sp.speaker: Pre.
sp.l: And what ſay you now, neighbour Turfe?

sp.speaker: Tur.
sp.l: I put it
Ene to your worſhips bitterment, hab, nab.
I ſhall have a chance o' the dice for't, I hope, let 'hem ene run: And

sp.speaker: Pre.
sp.l: Faith then Ile pray you, 'cauſe he is my neighbour,
To take a hundred pound, and give him day.

sp.speaker: Hug.
sp.l: Saint Valentines day, I will, this very day,
Before Sunne ſet: my bond is forfeit elſe.

sp.speaker: Tur.
sp.l: Where will you ha' it paid?

sp.speaker: Hug.
sp.l: Faith, I am a ſtranger
Here i' the countrey: Know you Chanon Hugh,
The Vicar of Pancrace?

sp.speaker: Tur.
sp.l: Yes, wee who not him?

sp.speaker: Hug.
sp.l: Ile make him my Attorney to receive it,
And give you a diſcharge.

sp.speaker: Tur.
sp.l: Whom ſhall I ſend for't?

sp.speaker: Pre.
sp.l: Why, if you pleaſe, ſend Metaphore my Clarke.
And Turfe, I much commend thy willingneſſe;
It's argument of thy integrity.

sp.speaker: Tur.
sp.l: But, my integrity ſhall be my zelfe ſtill:
Good Mr. Metaphore, give my wife this key;
And doe but whiſper it into her hand:
(She knowes it well inow) bid her, by that
Deliver you the two zeal'd bags o' ſilver,
That lie i' the corner o' the cup-bord, ſtands
At my bed-ſide, they' are viftie pound a peece;
And bring 'hem to your Maſter.

sp.speaker: Met.
sp.l: If I prove not
As juſt a Carrier as my friend Tom Long was,
Then call me his curtall, change my name of Miles,
To Guile's, Wile's, Pile's, Bile's, or the fouleſt name
You can deviſe, to crambe with, for ale.

sp.speaker: Hug.
sp.l: Come hither Miles, bring by that token, too,
Faire Awdrey; ſay her father ſent for her:
Say Clay is found, and waits at Pancrace Church,
Where I attend to marry them in haſte.
For (by this meanes) Miles I may ſay't to thee,
Thy Maſter muſt to Awdrey married be.
But not a word but mum: goe get thee gone;
Be warie of thy charge, and keepe it cloſe.

sp.speaker: Met.
sp.l: O ſuper-dainty Chanon! Vicar in cóney,
Make no delay, Miles, but a way.
And bring the wench, and money.

sp.speaker: Hug.
sp.l: Now Sir, I ſee you meant but honeſtly;
And, but that buſines cals me hence away,
I would not leave you, till the ſunne were lower.
But Mr. Juſtice, one word, Sir, with you.
By the ſame token, is your Miſtris ſent for
By Metaphore your Clarke, as from her Father.
Who when ſhe comes, Ile marry her to you,
Vnwitting to this Turfe, who ſhall attend
Me at the parſonage. This was my plot:
Which I muſt now make good; turne Chanon, againe,
In my ſquare cap. I humbly take my leave.

sp.speaker: Pre.
sp.l: Adieu, good Captaine. Truſt me, neighbour Turfe,
He ſeemes to be a ſober Gentleman:
But this diſtreſſe hath ſomewhat ſtir'd his patience.
And men, you know, in ſuch extremities,
Apt not themſelves to points of courteſie;
I'am glad you ha' made this end.

sp.speaker: Tur.
sp.l: You ſtood my friend:
I thanke your Juſtice-worſhip; pray you be
Prezent anone, at tendring o' the money,
And zee me have a diſcharge: Vor I ha' no craft
I' your Law quiblins.

sp.speaker: Pre.
sp.l: Ile ſecure you, neighbour.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: Indeed, there is a woundy luck in names, Sirs,
And a maine myſterie, an' a man knew where.
To vind it. My God-ſires name, Ile tell you,
Was In-and-In Shittle, and a Weaver he was,
And it did fit his craft: for ſo his Shittle
Went in, and in, ſtill: this way, and then that way.
And he nam'd me, In-and In Medlay: which ſerves
A Joyners craft, bycauſe that wee doe lay
Things in and in, in our worke. But, I am truly
Architectonicus profeſſor, rather:
That is (as one would zay) an Architect.

sp.speaker: Cle.
sp.l: As I am a Varrier, and a Viſicarie:
Horſe-ſmith of Hamſted, and the whole Towne Leach—.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: Yes, you ha' done woundy cures, Goſſip Clench.

sp.speaker: Cle.
sp.l: An' I can zee the ſtale once, through a Vrine-hole,
Ile give a ſhrew'd gheſſe, be it man, or beaſt.
I cur'd an Ale-wife once, that had the ſtaggers
Worſe then five horſes, without rowelling.
My God-phere was a Rabian, or a Iew,
(You can tell D'oge!) They call'd un Doctor Raſi.

sp.speaker: Scr.
sp.l: One Raſis was a great Arabick Doctor.

sp.speaker: Cle.
sp.l: Hee was King Harry's Doctor, and my God-phere.

sp.speaker: Pan.
sp.l: Mine was a merry Greeke, To-Pan, of Twyford:
A joviall Tinker, and a ſtopper of holes;
Who left me mettall-man of Belſiſe, his heire.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: But what was yours D'oge?

sp.speaker: Scr.
sp.l: Vaith, I cannot tell
If mine were kyrſind, or no. But, zure hee had
A kyrſin name, that he left me, Diogenes.
A mighty learned man, but peſt'lence poore.
Vor, h'had no houſe, ſave an old Tub, to dwell in,
(I vind that in records) and ſtill he turn'd it
I' the winds teeth, as't blew on his back-ſide,
And there they would lie rowting one at other,
A weeke, ſometimes.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: Thence came A Tale of a Tub;
And the virſt Tale of a Tub, old D'ogenes Tub.

sp.speaker: Scr.
sp.l: That was avore Sir Peter Tub, or his Lady.

sp.speaker: Pan.
sp.l: I, or the Squire their ſonne, Tripoli Tub.

sp.speaker: Cle.
sp.l: The Squire is a fine Gentleman!

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: He is more:
A Gentleman and a halfe; almoſt a Knight;
Within zixe inches: That's his true meaſure.

sp.speaker: Cle.
sp.l: Zure, you can gage 'hun.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: To a ſtreake, or leſſe:
I know his d'ameters, and circumference:
A Knight is ſixe diameters; and a Squire
Is vive, and zomewhat more: I know't by compaſſe,
And skale of man. I have upo' my rule here,
The juſt perportions of a Knight, a Squire;
With a tame Juſtice, or an Officer, rampant,
Vpo' the bench, from the high Conſtable
Downe to the Head-borough, or Tithing-man;
Or meaneſt Miniſter o'the peace, God ſave 'un.

sp.speaker: Pan.
sp.l: Why, you can tell us by the Squire, Neighbour,
Whence he is call'd a Conſtable, and whaffore.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: No, that's a booke-caſe: Scriben can doe that.
That's writing and reading, and records.

sp.speaker: Scr.
sp.l: Two words,
Cyning and Staple, make a Conſtable:
As wee'd ſay, A hold, or ſtay for the King.

sp.speaker: Cle.
sp.l: All Conſtables are truly Iohn's for the King,
What ere their names are; be they Tony, or Roger.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: And all are ſworne, as vingars o' one hand,
To hold together 'gainſt the breach o' the peace;
The High Conſtable is the Thumbe, as one would zay,
The hold-faſt o' the reſt.

sp.speaker: Pan.
sp.l: Pray luck he ſpeed
Well i' the buſines, betweene Captaine Thums,
And him.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: 'Ile warrant 'un for a groat:
I have his meaſures here in Rithmetique.
How he ſhould beare un ſelfe in all the lines
Of's place, and office: Let's zeeke 'un out.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Hilts, how do'ſt thou like o' this our good dayes worke?

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: As good ene nere a whit, as nere the better.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Shall we to Pancridge, or to Kentiſh-Towne, Hilts?

sp.speaker: Hit.
sp.l: Let Kentiſh-Towne, or Pancridge come to us,
If either will: I will goe home againe.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Faith Basket, our ſucceſſe hath beene but bad,
And nothing proſpers, that wee undertake;
For we can neither meet with Clay, nor Awdrey,
The Chanon Hugh, nor Turfe the Conſtable:
We are like men that wander in ſtrange woods,
And looſe our ſelves in ſearch of them wee ſeeke.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: This was becauſe wee roſe on the wrong ſide:
But as I am now here, juſt in the mid-way,
Ile zet my ſword on the pommell, and that line
The point valles too, wee'll take. whether it be
To Kentiſh-Towne, the Church, or home againe.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Enter Metaphor. Stay, ſtay thy hand: here's Juſtice Brambles Clarke,
The unlucky Hare hath croſt us all this day.
Ile ſtand aſide whilſt thou pump'ſt out of him
His buſines, Hilts; and how hee's now employed.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: Let mee alone? Ile uſe him in his kind.

sp.speaker: Met.
sp.l: Oh for a Pad-horſe, Pack-horſe, or a Poſt-horſe,
To beare me on his neck, his back, or his croupe!
I am as weary with running, as a Mil-horſe
That hath led the Mill once, twice, thrice about,
After the breath hath beene out of his body.
I could get up upon a pannier, a pannell,
Or, to ſay truth, a very Pack-ſadle,
Till all my honey were turn'd into gall;
And I could • it in the ſeat no longer,
Oh the legs of a lackey now, or a foot-man,
Who is the Surbater of a Clarke currant,
And the confounder of his treſleſſe dormant.
But who have we here, juſt in the nick?

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: I am neither nick, nor in the nick: therefore
You lie Sir Metaphor.

sp.speaker: Met.
sp.l: Lye? how?

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: Lye ſo Sir.

sp.speaker: Met.
sp.l: He ſtrikes up his heeles. I lye not yet i' my throat.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: Thou ly'ſt o' the ground.
Do'ſt thou know me?

sp.speaker: Met.
sp.l: Yes, I did know you too late.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: What is my name then?

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: Basket? what?

sp.speaker: Met.
sp.l: Basket, the Great—

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: The Great? what?

sp.speaker: Met.
sp.l: Lubber—
I ſhould ſay Lover, of the Squire his Maſter.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: Great is my patience, to forbeare thee thus,
Thou Scrape-hill, Skoundrell, and thou skum of man;
Vncivill, orenge-tawny-coated Clarke:
Thou cam'ſt but halfe a thing into the world,
And waſt made up of patches, parings, ſhreds:
Thou, that when laſt thou wert put out of ſervice,
Travaild'ſt to Hamſted Heath, on an Aſh-we'nsday,
Where thou didſt ſtand ſixe weekes the Iack of Lent,
For boyes to hoorle, three throwes a penny, at thee,
To make thee a purſe: Seeſt thou this, bold bright blade?
As minc'd meat for a pie. Ile ſet thee in earth
All ſave thy head, and thy right arme at liberty,
To keepe thy hat off, while I queſtion thee,
What? why? and whether thou wert going now
With a face, ready to breake out with buſines?
And tell me truly, leſt I daſh't in peeces.

sp.speaker: Met.
sp.l: Then Basket put thy ſmiter up, and heare;
I dare not tell the truth to a drawne ſword.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: 'Tis ſheath'd, ſtand up, ſpeake without feare, or wit.

sp.speaker: Met.
sp.l: I know not what they meane; but Conſtable Turfe
Sends here his key; for monies in his cubbard
Which he muſt pay the Captaine, that was rob'd
This morning. Smell you nothing?

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: No, not I;
Thy breeches yet are honeſt.

sp.speaker: Met.
sp.l: As my mouth.
Doe you not ſmell a rat? I tell you truth,
I thinke all's knavery: For the Chanon whiſper'd
Me in the eare, when Turfe had gi'n me his key,
By the ſame token to bring Mrs. Awdrey,
As ſent for thither; and to ſay Iohn Clay
Is found, which is indeed to get the wench
Forth for my Maſter, who is to be married,
When ſhe comes there: The Chanon has his rules
Ready, and all there to diſpatch the matter.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Now on my life, this is the Chanon's plot!
Miles, I have heard all thy diſcourſe to Basket.
Wilt thou be true, and Ile reward thee well,
To make me happy, in my Miſtris Awdrey?

sp.speaker: Met.
sp.l: Your worſhip ſhall diſpoſe of Metaphore,
Through all his parts, ene from the ſole o' the head,
To the crowne o' the foot, to manage of your ſervice.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Then doe thy meſſage to the Miſtris Turfe,
Tell her thy token, bring the money hither,
And likewiſe take young Awdrey to thy charge:
Which done, here, Metaphore, wee will attend,
And intercept thee. And for thy reward,
You two ſhall ſhare the money; I the Maid:
If any take offence, Ile make all good.

sp.speaker: Met.
sp.l: But ſhall I have halfe the money Sir, in faith?

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: I on my Squire-ſhip, ſhalt thou: and my land.

sp.speaker: Met.
sp.l: Then, if I make not, Sir, the cleanlieſt ſcuſe
To get her hither, and be then as carefull
To keepe her for you, as't were for my ſelfe:
Downe o' your knees, and pray that honeſt Miles
May breake his neck ere he get ore two ſtiles.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Make haſte then: we will wait here thy returne.
This luck unlook'd for, hath reviv'd my hopes,
Which were oppreſt with a darke melancholly.
In happy time, we linger'd on the way,
To meet theſe ſummons of a better ſound,
Which are the eſſence of my ſoules content.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: This heartleſſe fellow; ſhame to ſerving-men;
Staine of all livories; what feare makes him doe!
How ſordid, wretched, and unworthy things;
Betray his Maſters ſecrets, ope the cloſet
Of his deviſes, force the fooliſh Juſtice,
Make way for your Love, plotting of his owne:
Like him that digs a trap, to catch another,
And falls into't himſelfe!

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: So wou'd I have it.
And hope 'twill prove a jeſt to twit the Juſtice with.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: But that this poore white-liver'd Rogue ſhould do't?
And meerely out of feare?

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: And hope of money, Hilts.
A valiant man will nible at that bait.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: Who, but a foole, will refuſe money proffer'd?

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: And ſent by ſo good chance. Pray heaven he ſpeed.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: If he come empty-headed, let him count
To goe back empty-headed; Ile not leave him
So much of braine in's pate, with pepper and vineger,
To be ſerv'd in for ſawce, to a Calves head.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Thou ſerv'ſt him rightly, Hilts.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: Ile ſeale az much
With my hand, as I dare ſay now with my tongue;
But if you get the Laſſe from Dargiſon,
What will you doe with her?

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Wee'll thinke o' that
When once wee have her in poſſeſſion, Governour.

sp.speaker: Pup.
sp.l: You ſee wee truſt you, Mr. Metaphore,
With Mrs. Awdrey: pray you uſe her well,
As a Gentle-woman ſhould be us'd. For my part,
I doe incline a little to the ſerving-man;
Wee have beene of a coat—I had one like yours:
Till it did play me ſuch a ſleeveleſſe errand,
As I had nothing where to put mine armes in,
And then I threw it off. Pray you goe before her,
Serving-man-like: and ſee that your noſe drop not.
As for example; you ſhall ſee me: marke,
How I goe afore her. So doe you: ſweet Miles,
She for her owne part, is a woman cares not
What man can doe unto her, in the way
Of honeſty, and good manners. So farewell
Faire Mrs. Awdrey: Farewell Mr. Miles.
I ha' brought you thus farre, onward o'your way:
I muſt goe back now to make cleane the roomes,
Where my good Lady has beene. Pray you commend mee
To Bride-groome Clay; and bid him beare up ſtiffe.

sp.speaker: Met.
sp.l: Thanke you good Hanniball Puppy; I ſhall fit
The leg of your commands, with the ſtraight buskins
Of diſpatch preſently.

sp.speaker: Pup.
sp.l: Farewell fine Metaphore.

sp.speaker: Met.
sp.l: Come gentle Miſtris, will you pleaſe to walke?

sp.speaker: Awd.
sp.l: I love not to be led: I'd goe alone.

sp.speaker: Met.
sp.l: Let not the mouſe of my good meaning, Lady,
Be ſnap'd up in the trap of your ſuſpition,
To looſe the taile there, either of her truth,
Or ſwallow'd by the Cat of miſconſtruction.

sp.speaker: Awd.
sp.l: You are too finicall forme; ſpeake plaine Sir.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Welcome againe my Awdrey: welcome Love:
You ſhall with me; in faith deny me not.
I cannot brook the ſecond hazzard Miſtris.

sp.speaker: Awd.
sp.l: Forbeare Squire Tub, as mine owne mother ſayes;
I am not for your mowing. Youle be flowne
Ere I be fledge.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: Haſt thou the money Miles?

sp.speaker: Met.
sp.l: Here are two bags, there's fiftie pound in each.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Nay Awdrey, I poſſeſſe you for this time:
Sirs; Take that coyne betweene you, and divide it.
My pretty ſweeting give me now the leave
To challenge love, and marriage at your hands.

sp.speaker: Awd.
sp.l: Now, out upon you, are you not aſham'd?
What will my Lady ſay? In faith I thinke
She was at our houſe: And I thinke ſhee ask'd for you:
And I thinke ſhe hit me i' th' teeth with you,
I thanke her Ladiſhip, and I thinke ſhe meanes
Not to goe hence, till ſhe has found you. How ſay you?

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Was then my Lady Mother at your houſe?
Let's have a word aſide.

sp.speaker: Awd.
sp.l: Yes, twenty words.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: 'Tis ſtrange, a motion, but I know not what,
Comes in my mind, to leave the way to Totten,
And turne to Kentiſh-Towne, againe my journey:
And ſee my ſonne Pol-marten with his Awdrey:
Ere while we left her at her fathers houſe:
And hath he thence remov'd her in ſuch haſte!
What ſhall I doe? ſhall I ſpeake faite, or chide?

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: Madam, your worthy ſonne, with dutious care,
Can governe his affections: Rather then
Breake off their conference ſome other way,
Pretending ignorance of what you know.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: And this all, faire

sp.speaker: Awdrey:
sp.l: I am thine.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: Mine you were once, though ſcarcely now your own.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: 'Slid my Lady! my Lady!

sp.speaker: Met.
sp.l: Is this my Lady bright?

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Madam, you tooke me now a little tardie.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: At prayers, I thinke you were: what, ſo devout
Of late, that you will ſhrive you to all Confeſſors
You meet by chance? Come, goe with me, good Squire,
And leave your linnen: I have now a buſines,
And of importance, to impart unto you.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Madam, I pray you, ſpare me but an houre;
Pleaſe you to walke before, I follow you.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: It muſt be now, my buſines lies this way.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Will not an houre hence, Madam, excuſe me?

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: Squire, theſe excuſes argue more your guilt.
You have ſome new device now, to project,
Which the poore Tile-man ſcarce will thanke you for.
What? will you goe?

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: I ha' tane a charge upon me,
To ſee this Maid conducted to her Father,
Who, with the Chanon Hugh, ſtaies her at Pancrace,
To ſee her married to the ſame Iohn Clay.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: Tis very well; but Squire take you no care.
Ile ſend Pol-marten with her, for that office.
You ſhall along with me; it is decreed.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: I have a little buſines, with a friend Madam.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: That friend ſhall ſtay for you, or you for him.
Pol-marten; Take the Maiden to your care;
Commend me to her Father.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: I will follow you.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: Tut, tell not me of following.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Ile but ſpeake
A word.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: No whiſpering: you forget your ſelfe,
And make your love too palpable: A Squire?
And thinke ſo meanely? fall upon a Cow-ſhard?
You know my mind. Come, Ile to Turfe's houſe,
And ſee for Dido, and our Valentine.

They all goe out but Pol-marten and Awdrey.
Pol-marten, looke to your charge; Ile looke to mine.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: I ſmile to thinke after ſo many proffers
This Maid hath had, ſhe now ſhould fall to me:
That I ſhould have her in my cuſtody:
Twere but a mad trick to make the eſſay,
And jumpe a match with her immediately:
She's faire, and handſome: and ſhee's rich enough:
Both time, and place miniſter faire occaſion:
Have at it then: Faire Lady, can you love?

sp.speaker: Awd.
sp.l: No Sir, what's that?

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: A toy, which women uſe.

sp.speaker: Awd.
sp.l: If't be a toy, it's good to play withall.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: Wee will not ſtand diſcourſing o'the toy:
The way is ſhort pleaſe you to prov't Miſtris?

sp.speaker: Awd.
sp.l: If you doe meane to ſtand ſo long upon it;
I pray you let me give it a ſhort cut, Sir.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: It's thus, faire Maid: Are you diſpos'd to marry?

sp.speaker: Awd.
sp.l: You are diſpos'd to aske.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: Are you to grant?

sp.speaker: Awd.
sp.l: Nay, now I ſee you are diſpos'd indeed.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: I ſee the wench wants but a little wit;
And that defect her wealth may well ſupply:
In plaine termes, tell me, Will you have me Awdrey?

sp.speaker: Awd.
sp.l: In as plaine termes, I tell you who would ha' me.
Iohn Clay would ha' me, but he hath too hard hands;
I like not him: beſides, hee is a thiefe.
And Juſtice Bramble, he would faine ha' catch'd me:
But the young Squire, hee, rather then his life,
Would ha' me yet; and make me a Lady, hee ſayes,
And be my Knight; to doe me true Knights ſervice,
Before his Lady Mother. Can you make me
A Lady, would I ha' you?

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: I can gi' you
A ſilken Gowne, and a rich Petticoat:
And a french Hood. All fooles love to be brave:
I find her humour, and I will purſue it.

div.head: ACT IIII. SCENE VI.
Lady. D. Turfe. Squire Tub. Hilts. Puppy. Clay.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: And as I told thee, ſhee was intercepted
By the Squire here, my ſonne: and this bold Ruffin
His man, who ſafely would have carried her
Vnto her Father; and the Chanon Hugh;
But for more care of the ſecurity,
My Huiſher hath her now, in his grave charge.

sp.speaker: D. Tur.
sp.l: Now on my faith, and holy-dom, we are
Beholden to your worſhip. She's a Girle,
A fooliſh Girle, and ſoone may tempted be:
But if this day paſſe well once ore her head,
Ile wiſh her truſt to her ſelfe. For I have beene
A very mother to her, though I ſay it.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Madam, 'tis late, and Pancridge is i' your way:
I thinke your Ladiſhip forgets your ſelfe.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: Your mind runs much on Pancridge. Well, young Squire,
The black Oxe never trod yet O your foot:
Theſe idle Phant'ſies will forſake you one day.
Come Mrs. Turfe, will you goe take a walke
Over the fields to Pancridge, to your husband?

sp.speaker: D. Tur.
sp.l: Madam, I had beene there an houre agoe:
But that I waited on my man Ball Puppy.
What Ball I ſay? I thinke the idle •• ouch
Be falne aſleepe i' the barne, he ſtayes ſo long.

sp.speaker: Pup.
sp.l: Sattin, i' the name of velvet Sattin, Dame!
The Divell! O the Divell is in the barne:
Helpe, helpe, a legion—Spirit legion,
Is in the barne! in every ſtraw a Divell.

sp.speaker: Tur.
sp.l: Why do'ſt thou bawle ſo Puppy? Speake, what ailes thee?

sp.speaker: Pup.
sp.l: My name's Ball Puppy, I ha' ſeene the Divell
Among the ſtraw: O for a Croſſe! a Collop
Of Friar Bacon, or a conjuring ſtick
Of Doctor Fauſtus! Spirits are in the barne.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: How! Spirits in the barne? Basket, goe ſee.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: Sir, an' you were my Maſter ten times over,
And Squire to boot; I know, and you ſhall pardon me:
Send me 'mong Divels? I zee you love me not:
Hell be at their game: Ile not trouble them.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Goe ſee; I warrant thee there's no ſuch matter.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: An' they were Giants, 't were another matter.
But Divells! No, if I be torne in peeces,
What is your warrant worth? Ile ſee the Feind
Set fire o' the barne, ere I come there.

sp.speaker: D. Tur.
sp.l: Now all Zaints bleſſe us, and if he be there,
He is an ugly ſpright, I warrant.

sp.speaker: Pup.
sp.l: As ever
Held fleſh-hooke, Dame, or handled fire-forke rather:
They have put me in a ſweet pickle, Dame:
But that my Lady-Valentine ſmels of muske,
I ſhould be aſham'd to preſſe into this preſence.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: Basket, I pray thee ſee what is the miracle!

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Come, goe with me: Ile lead. Why ſtand'ſt thou man?

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: Cocks pretious Maſter, you are not mad indeed?
You will not goe to hell before your time?

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Why art thou thus afraid?

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: No, not afraid:
But by your leave, Ile come no neare the barne.

sp.speaker: Tur.
sp.l: Puppy! wilt thou goe with me?

sp.speaker: Pup.
sp.l: How? goe with you▪
Whither, into the Barne? To whom, the Divell?
Or to doe what there? to be torne 'mongſt 'hum?
Stay for my Maſter, the High Conſtable,
Or In-and-In, the Head-borough; let them goe,
Into the Barne with warrant, ſeize the Feind;
And ſet him in the ſtocks for his ill rule:
'Tis not for me that am but fleſh and blood,
To medle with 'un. Vor I cannot, nor I wu' not.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: I pray thee Tripoly, looke, what is the matter?

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: That ſhall I Madam.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: Heaven protect my Maſter.
I tremble every joynt till he be back.

sp.speaker: Pup.
sp.l: Now, now, even now they are tearing him in peeces▪
Now are they toſſing of his legs, and armes,
Like Loggets at a Peare-tree: Ile to the hole,
Peepe in, and looke whether he lives or dies.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: I would not be i' my Maſters coat for thouſands.

sp.speaker: Pup.
sp.l: Then pluck it off, and turne thy ſelfe away.
O the Divell! the Divell! the Divell!

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: Where man? where?

sp.speaker: D. Tur.
sp.l: Alas that ever wee were borne. So neere too?

sp.speaker: Pup.
sp.l: The Squire hath him in his hand, and leads him
Out by the Collar.

sp.speaker: D. Tur.
sp.l: O this is Iohn Clay.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: Iohn Clay at Pancrace, is there to be married.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: This was the ſpirit reveld i' the Barne.

sp.speaker: Pup.
sp.l: The Divell hee was: was this he was crawling
Among the Wheat-ſtraw? Had it beene the Barley,
I ſhould ha' tane him for the Divell in drinke;
The Spirit of the Bride-ale: But poore Iohn,
Tame Iohn of Clay, that ſticks about the bung-hole—

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: If this be all your Divell, I would take
In hand to conjure him: But hell take me
If ere I come in a right Divels walke,
If I can keepe me out on't.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Well meant Hilts.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: But how came Clay thus hid here i' the ſtraw,
When newes was brought, to you all hee was at Pancridge;
And you beleev'd it?

sp.speaker: D. Tur.
sp.l: Juſtice Brambles man
Told me ſo, Madam: And by that ſame token,
And other things, he had away my Daughter,
And two ſeal'd bags of money.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: Where's the Squire?
Is hee gone hence?

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: H' was here Madam, but now.

sp.speaker: Clay.
sp.l: Is the Huy and Cry paſt by?

sp.speaker: Pup.
sp.l: I, I, Iohn Clay.

sp.speaker: Clay.
sp.l: And am I out of danger to be hang'd?

sp.speaker: Pup.
sp.l: Hang'd Iohn? yes ſure; unleſſe, as with the Proverbe,
You meane to make the choice of your owne gallowes.

sp.speaker: Cla.
sp.l: Nay, then all's well, hearing your newes Ball Pupy,
You ha' brought from Paddington, I ene ſtole home here,
And thought to hide me, in the Barne ere ſince.

sp.speaker: Pup.
sp.l: O wonderfull! and newes was brought us here,
You were at Pancridge, ready to be married.

sp.speaker: Cla.
sp.l: No faith, I nere was furder then the Barne.

sp.speaker: D. Tur.
sp.l: Haſte Puppy. Call forth Miſtris Dido Wiſpe,
My Ladies Gentle-woman, to her Lady;
And call your ſelfe forth, and a couple of maids,
To waite upon me: we are all undone!
My Lady is undone! her fine young ſonne,
The Squire is got away.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: Haſte, haſte, good Valentine.

sp.speaker: D. Tur.
sp.l: And you Iohn Clay; you are undone too! All!
My husband is undone, by a true key,
But a falſe token: And my ſelfe's undone,
By parting with my Daughter, who'll be married
To ſome body, that ſhe ſhould not, if wee haſte not.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: I Pray thee good Pol-marten, ſhew thy diligence,
And faith in both: Get her, but ſo diſguis'd,
The Chanon may not know her, and leave me
To plot the reſt: I will expect thee here.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: You ſhall Squire. Ile performe it with all care,
If all my Ladies Ward-robe will diſguiſe her.
Come Miſtris Awdrey.

sp.speaker: Awd.
sp.l: Is the Squire gone?

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: Hee'll meet us by and by, where he appointed:
You ſhall be brave anone, as none ſhall know you.

sp.speaker: Cle.
sp.l: I wonder, where the Queenes High Conſtable is!
I veare, they ha' made 'hun away.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: No zure; The Juſtice
Dare not conzent to that. Hee'll zee 'un forth comming.

sp.speaker: Pan.
sp.l: He muſt, vor wee can all take corpulent oath,
Wee zaw 'un goe in there.

sp.speaker: Scr.
sp.l: I, upon record!
The Clock dropt twelve at Maribone.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: You are right, D'oge!
Zet downe to a minute, now 'tis a 'moſt vowre.

sp.speaker: Cle.
sp.l: Here comes Squire Tub.

sp.speaker: Scr.
sp.l: And's Governour, Mr. Basket.
Hilts, doe you know 'hun, a valiant wiſe vellow!
Az tall a man on his hands, as goes on veet.
Bleſſe you Maſs' Basket.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: Thanke you good D'oge.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: who's that?

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: D'oge Scriben, the great Writer Sir of Chalcot.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: And, who the reſt?

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: The wiſeſt heads o'the hundred.
Medlay the Ioyner, Head-borough of Iſlington,
Pan of Belſize, and Clench the Leach of Hamſted.
The High Conſtables Counſell, here of Finsbury,

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Prezent me to 'hem, Hilts, Squire Tub of Totten.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: Wiſe men of Finsbury: make place for a Squire,
I bring to your acquaintance, Tub of Totten.
Squire Tub, my Maſter, loves all men of vertue.
And longs (az one would zay) till he be one on you.

sp.speaker: Cle.
sp.l: His worſhip's wel'cun to our company:
Would't were wiſer for 'hun.

sp.speaker: Pan.
sp.l: Here be ſome on us,
Are call'd the witty men, over a hundred;

sp.speaker: Scr.
sp.l: And zome a thouſand, when the Muſter day comes.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: I long (as my man Hilts ſaid, and my Governour)
To be adopt in your ſociety.
Can any man make a Maſque here i' this company?

sp.speaker: Pan.
sp.l: A Maſque, what's that?

sp.speaker: Scr.
sp.l: A mumming, or a ſhew.
With vizards, and fine clothes.

sp.speaker: Cle.
sp.l: A diſguiſe, neighbour,
Is the true word: There ſtands the man, can do't Sir.
Medlay the Joyner, In-and-In of Iſlington,
The onely man at a diſguize in Midleſex.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: But who ſhall write it?

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: Scriben, the great Writer.

sp.speaker: Scr.
sp.l: Hee'll do't alone Sir, He will joyne with no man:
Though he be a Joyner, in deſigne he cals it.
He muſt be ſole Inventer: In-and-In.
Drawes with no other in's project, hee'll tell you,
It cannot elſe be feazeable, or conduce:
Thoſe are his ruling words? Pleaze you to heare 'hun?

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Yes Mr. In-and-In, I have heard of you;

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: I can doe nothing, I.

sp.speaker: Cle.
sp.l: Hee can doe all Sir.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: They'll tell you ſo.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: I'ld have a toy preſented,
A Tale of a Tub, a ſtorie of my ſelfe,
You can expreſſe a Tub.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: If it conduce
To the deſigne, what ere is feazeable:
I can expreſſe a Waſh-houſe (If need be)
With a whole pedigree of Tubs.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: No, one
Will be enough to note our name, and family:
Squire Tub of Totten, and to ſhew my adventures
This very day. I'ld have it in Tubs-Hall,
At Totten-Court, my Ladie Mothers houſe,
My houſe indeed, for I am heire to it.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: If I might ſee the place, and had ſurvey'd it;
I could ſay more: For all Invention, Sir,
Comes by degrees, and on the view of nature;
A world of things, concurre to the deſigne,
Which make it feazible, if Art conduce.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: You ſay well, witty Mr. In-and-In.
How long ha' you ſtudied Ingine?

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: Since I firſt
Ioyn'd, or did in-lay in wit, ſome vorty yeare.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: A pretty time! Basket, goe you and waite
On Maſter In-and-In to Totten-Court,
And all the other wiſe Maſters; ſhew 'hem the Hall:
And taſte the language of the buttery to 'hem;
Let 'hem ſee all the Tubs about the houſe,
That can raiſe matter, till I come—which ſhall be a
Within an houre at leaſt.

sp.speaker: Cle.
sp.l: It will be glorious,
If In-and-In will undertake it, Sir:
He has a monſtrous medlay wit o' his owne.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Spare for no coſt, either in boords, or hoops,
To architect your Tub: Ha' you nere a Cooper
Or old Iohn Haywood, call him to you, to helpe.

sp.speaker: Scr.
sp.l: He ſcornes the motion, truſt to him alone.

head.hi: Lady. Tub. D. Tur. Clay. Puppy. Wiſpe. Preamble. Turfe.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: O, here's the Squire! you ſlip'd us finely ſonne!
Theſe manners to your Mother, will commend you;
But in an other age, not this: well Tripoly,
Your Father, good Sir Peter (reſt his bones)
Would not ha done this: where's my Huiſher Martin?
And your faire Mrs. Awdrey?

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: I not ſee'hem,
No creature, but the foure wiſe Maſters here,
Of Finsbury Hundred, came to cry their Conſtable,
Who they doe ſay is loſt.

sp.speaker: D. Tur.
sp.l: My husband loſt?
And my fond Daughter loſt? I feare mee too.
Where is your Gentleman, Madam? Poore Iohn Clay,
Thou haſt loſt thy Awdrey.

sp.speaker: Cla.
sp.l: I ha' loſt my wits,
My little wits, good Mother; I am diſtracted.

sp.speaker: Pup.
sp.l: And I have loſt my Miſtris Dido Wiſpe,
Who frownes upon her Puppy, Hanniball.
Loſſe! loſſe on everyſide! a publike loſſe!
Loſſe o' my Maſter! loſſe of his Daughter! loſſe
Of Favour, Friends, my Miſtris! loſſe of all!

sp.speaker: Pre.
sp.l: What Cry is this?

sp.speaker: Tur.
sp.l: My man ſpeakes of ſome loſſe.

sp.speaker: Pup.
sp.l: My Maſter is found: Good luck, and't be thy will,
Light on us all.

sp.speaker: D. Tur.
sp.l: O husband, are you a live?
They ſaid you were loſt.

sp.speaker: Tur.
sp.l: Where's Juſtice Brambles Clarke?
Had he the money that I ſent for?

sp.speaker: D. Tur.
sp.l: Yes,
Two houres agoe, two fifty pounds in ſilver,
And Awdrey too.

sp.speaker: Tur.
sp.l: Why Awdrey? who ſent for her?

sp.speaker: D. Tur.
sp.l: You Maſter Turfe, the fellow ſaid.

sp.speaker: Tur.
sp.l: Hee lyed.
I am cozen'd, rob'd, undone: your man's a Thiefe,
And run away with my Daughter, Mr. Bramble,
And with my money.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: Neighbour Turfe have patience,
I can aſſure you that your Daughter is ſafe,
But for the monies I know nothing of.

sp.speaker: Tur.
sp.l: My money is my Daughter; and my Daughter
She is my money, Madam.

sp.speaker: Pre.
sp.l: I doe wonder
Your Ladiſhip comes to know any thing
In theſe affaires.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: Yes, Juſtice Bramble
I met the maiden i' the fields by chance,
I' the Squires company my ſonne: How hee
Lighted upon her, himſelfe beſt can tell.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: I intercepted her, as comming hither,
To her Father, who ſent for her, by Miles Metaphore,
Juſtice Preambles Clarke. And had your Ladiſhip
Not hindred it, I had paid fine Mr. Juſtice
For his young warrant, and new Purs'yvant,
He ſerv'd it by this morning.

sp.speaker: Pre.
sp.l: Know you that Sir?

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: You told me, Squire, a quite other tale,
But I beleev'd you not, which made me ſend
Awdrey another way, by my Pol-marten:
And take my journey back to Kentiſh-Towne,
Where we found Iohn Clay hidden i' the barne,
To ſcape the Huy and Cry; and here he is.

sp.speaker: Tur.
sp.l: Iohn Clay age'n! nay, then — ſet Cock a hoope:
I ha' loſt no Daughter, nor no money, Juſtice.
Iohn Clay ſhall pay. Ile looke to you now John.
Vaith out it muſt, as good at night, as morning.
I am ene as vull as a Pipers bag with joy,
Or a great Gun upon carnation day!
I could weepe Lions teares to ſee you Iohn.
'Tis but two viftie pounds I ha' ventur'd for you:
But now I ha' you, you ſhall pay whole hundred.
Run from your Burroughs, ſonne: faith ene be hang'd.
An' you once earth your ſelfe, Iohn, i' the barne,
I ha' no Daughter vor you: Who did verret 'hun.

sp.speaker: D. Tur.
sp.l: My Ladies ſonne, the Squire here, vetch'd 'hun out.
Puppy had put us all in ſuch a vright,
We thought the Devill was i' the barne; and no body
Durſt venture o' hun.

sp.speaker: Tur.
sp.l: I am now reſolv'd,
Who ſhall ha' my Daughter.

sp.speaker: D. Tur.
sp.l: Who?

sp.speaker: Tur.
sp.l: He beſt deſerves her.
Here comes the Vicar. Chanon Hugh, we ha' vound
Iohn Clay agen! the matter's all come round.

sp.speaker: Hugh.
sp.l: Is Metaphore return'd yet?

sp.speaker: Pre.
sp.l: All is turn'd
Here to confuſion: we ha' loſt our plot;
I feare my man is run away with the money,
And Clay is found, in whom old Turfe is ſure
To ſave his ſtake.

sp.speaker: Hug.
sp.l: What ſhall wee doe then Juſtice?

sp.speaker: Pre.
sp.l: The Bride was met i' the young Squires hands.

sp.speaker: Hug.
sp.l: And what's become of her?

sp.speaker: Pre.
sp.l: None here can tell.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Was not my Mothers man, Pol-marten, with you?
And a ſtrange Gentlewoman in his company,
Of late here, Chanon?

sp.speaker: Hug.
sp.l: Yes, and I diſpatch'd 'hem.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Diſpatch'd 'hem! how doe you meane?

sp.speaker: Hug.
sp.l: Why married 'hem.
As they deſir'd; But now.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: And doe you know
What you ha' done, Sir Hugh?

sp.speaker: Hug.
sp.l: No harme, I hope.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: You have ended all the Quarrell. Awdrey is married.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: Married! to whom?

sp.speaker: Tur.
sp.l: My Daughter Awdrey married,
And ſhe not know of it!

sp.speaker: D. Tur.
sp.l: Nor her Father, or Mother!

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: Whom hath ſhe married?

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Your Pol-marten, Madam.
A Groome was never dreamt of.

sp.speaker: Tur.
sp.l: Is he a man?

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: That he is Turfe, and a Gentleman, I ha' made him.

sp.speaker: D. Tur.
sp.l: Nay, an' he be a Gentleman, let her ſhift.

sp.speaker: Hug.
sp.l: She was ſo brave, I knew her not, I ſweare;
And yet I married her by her owne name.
But ſhe was ſo diſguis'd, ſo Lady-like;
I thinke ſhe did not know her ſelfe the while!
I married 'hem as a meere p •• re of ſtrangers:
And they gave out themſelves for ſuch.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: I wiſh 'hem
Much joy, as they have given me hearts eaſe.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Then Madam, Ile intreat you now remit
Your jealouſie of me; and pleaſe to take
All this good company home with you, to ſupper:
Wee'll have a merry night of it, and laugh.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: A right good motion, Squire; which I yeeld to:
And thanke them to accept it. Neighbour Turfe,
Ile have you merry, and your wife: And you,
Sir Hugh, be pardon'd this your happy error.
By Juſtice Preamble, your friend and patron.

sp.speaker: Pre.
sp.l: If the young Squire can pardon it, I doe.

sp.speaker: Pup.
sp.l: Stay my deare Dido, and good Vicar Hugh,
We have a buſines with you: In ſhort, this
If you dare knit another paire of ſtrangers,
Dido of Carthage, and her Countrey-man,
Stout Hanniball ſtands to't. I have ask'd conſent,
And ſhe hath granted.

sp.speaker: Hug.
sp.l: But ſaith Dido ſo?

sp.speaker: Did.
sp.l: From what Ball-Hanny hath ſaid, I dare not goe.

sp.speaker: Hug.
sp.l: Come in then, Ile diſpatch you. A good ſupper
Would not be loſt, good company, good diſcourſe;
But above all where wit hath any ſource.

head.hi: Pol-marten. Awdrey. Tub. Lady. Preamble. Turfe. D. Turfe. Clay.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: After the hoping of your pardon, Madam,
For many faults committed. Here my wife,
And I doe ſtand expecting your mild doome.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: I wiſh thee joy Pol-marten; and thy wife:
As much, Mrs. Pol-marten. Thou haſt trick'd her
Vp very fine, me thinkes.

sp.speaker: Pol.
sp.l: For that I made
Bold with your Ladiſhips Wardrobe, but have treſpaſs'd
Within the limits of your leave— I hope.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: I give her what ſhe weares. I know all women
Love to be fine. Thou haſt deſerv'd it of me:
I am extreamely pleas'd with thy good fortune.
Welcome good Juſtice Preamble; And Turfe,
Looke merrily on your Daughter: She has married
A Gentleman.

sp.speaker: Tur.
sp.l: So me thinkes; I dare not touch her
She is ſo fine: yet I will ſay, God bleſſe h •• .

sp.speaker: D. Tur.
sp.l: And I too, my fine Daughter. I could love her
Now, twice as well, as if Clay had her.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Come, come, my Mother is pleas'd. I pardon all,
Pol-marten in, and waite upon my Lady.
Welcome good Gheſts: ſee ſupper be ſerv'd in,
With all the plenty of the houſe, and worſhip.
I muſt conferre with Mr. In-and-In,
About ſome alterations in my Maſque;
Send Hilts out to me: Bid him bring the Councell
Of Finsbury hither. Ile have ſuch a night
Shall make the name of Totten-Court immortall:
And be recorded to poſterity.

div.head: ACT V. SCENE VII.
div.stage: Tub. Medlay. Clench. Pan. Scriben. Hilts.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: O Mr. In-and-In, what ha' you done?

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: Survey'd the place Sir, and deſign'd the ground,
Or ſtand ſtill of the worke: And this it is.
Firſt, I have fixed in the earth, a Tub;
And an old Tub, like a Salt-Peeter Tub,
Preluding by your Fathers name Sir Peeter,
And the antiquity of your houſe, and family,
Originall from Salt-Peeter.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Good y faith,
You ha' ſhewne reading, and antiquity here, Sir.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: I have a little knowledge in deſigne,
Which I can varie Sir to Infinite.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Ad Infinitum Sir you meane.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: I doe.
I ſtand not on my Latine, Ile invent,
But I muſt be alone then, joyn'd with no man.
This we doe call the Stand-ſtill of our worke.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Who are thoſe wee? you now joyn'd to your ſelfe.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: I meane my ſelfe ſtill, in the plurall number,
And out of this wee raiſe our Tale of a Tub.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: No, Mr. In-and-In, my Tale of a Tub.
By your leave, I am Tub, the Tale's of me,
And my adventures! I am Squire Tub,

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: But I the Author.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: The Worke-man Sir! the Artificer! I grant you.
So Skelton-Lawreat; was of Elinour Bumming:
But ſhe the ſubject of the Rout, and Tunning.

sp.speaker: Cle.
sp.l: He has put you to it, Neighbour In-and-In.

sp.speaker: Pan.
sp.l: Doe not diſpute with him, he ſtill will win.
That paies for all.

sp.speaker: Scr.
sp.l: Are you revis'd o'that?
A man may have wit, and yet put off his hat.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: Now, Sir this Tub, I will have capt with paper:
A fine oild Lanterne-paper, that we uſe.

sp.speaker: Pan.
sp.l: Yes every Barber, every Cutler has it.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: Which in it doth containe the light to the buſines.
And ſhall with the very vapour of the Candle,
Drive all the motions of our matter about:
As we preſent 'hem. For example, firſt
The worſhipfull Lady Tub.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Right worſhipfull,
I pray you, I am worſhipfull my ſelfe.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: Your Squire-ſhips Mother, paſſeth by (her Huiſher,
Mr. Pol-marten bareheaded before her)
In her velvet Gowne.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: But how ſhall the Spectators?
As it might be, I, or Hilts, know 'tis my Mother?
Or that Pol-marten there that walkes before her.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: O wee doe nothing, if we cleare not that.

sp.speaker: Cle.
sp.l: You ha' ſeene none of his workes Sir?

sp.speaker: Pan.
sp.l: All the poſtures
Of the train'd bands o'the Countrey.

sp.speaker: Scr.
sp.l: All their colours.

sp.speaker: Pan.
sp.l: And all their Captaines.

sp.speaker: Cle.
sp.l: All the Cries o' the Citie:
And all the trades i' their habits.

sp.speaker: Scr.
sp.l: He has his whiſtle
Of command: Seat of authority!
And virge to' interpret, tip'd with ſilver, Sir
You know not him.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Well, I will leave all to him:

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: Give me the briefe o' your ſubject. Leave the whole
State of the thing to me.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: Supper is ready, Sir.
My Lady cals for you.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Ile ſend it you in writing.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: Sir, I will render feazible, and facile,
What you expect.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Hilts, be't your care,
To ſee the Wiſe of Finsbury made welcome:
The Squire goes out. Let 'hem want nothing. Iz old Roſin ſent for?

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: Hee's come within.

sp.speaker: Scri.
sp.l: Lord! what a world of buſines
The Squire diſpatches!

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: Hee is a learned man:
I thinke there are but vew o' the Innes o' Court,
The reſt follow. Or the Innes o' Chancery like him.

sp.speaker: Cle.
sp.l: Care to fit 'un then.

sp.speaker: Iac.
sp.l: Yonder's another wedding, Maſter Basket,
Brought in by Vicar Hugh.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: what are they, Iack?

sp.speaker: Iac.
sp.l: The High Conſtables Man, Ball Hanny; and Mrs. Wiſpes,
Our Ladies woman.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: And are the Table merry?

sp.speaker: Iac.
sp.l: There's a young Tile-maker makes all laugh;
He will not eate his meat, but cryes at th' boord,
He ſhall be hang'd.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: He has loſt his wench already:
As good be hang'd.

sp.speaker: Iac.
sp.l: Was ſhe that is Pol-marten,
Our fellowes Miſtris, wench to that ſneake-Iohn?

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: I faith, Black Iack, he ſhould have beene her Bride-groome:
But I muſt goe to waite o' my wiſe Maſters.
Iack, you ſhall waite on me, and ſee the Maske anone:
I am halfe Lord Chamberlin, i' my Maſters abſence.

sp.speaker: Iac.
sp.l: Shall wee have a Maſque? Who makes it?

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: The Maker of Iſlington: Come goe with me
To the ſage ſentences of Finsbury.

sp.speaker: Gro. 1.
sp.l: Come, give us in the great Chaire, for my Lady;
And ſet it there: and this for Juſtice Bramble.

sp.speaker: Gro. 2.
sp.l: This for the Squire my Maſter, on the right hand.

sp.speaker: Gro. 1.
sp.l: And this for the High Conſtable.

sp.speaker: Gro. 2.
sp.l: This his wife.

sp.speaker: Gro. 1.
sp.l: Then for the Bride, and Bride-groome, here Pol-marten.

sp.speaker: Gro. 2.
sp.l: And ſhe Pol-marten, army Ladies feet.

sp.speaker: Gro. 1.
sp.l: Right.

sp.speaker: Gro. 2.
sp.l: And beſide them Mr. Hanniball Puppy.

sp.speaker: Gro. 1.
sp.l: And his ſhee Puppy, Mrs. Wiſpe that was:
Here's all are in the note.

sp.speaker: Gro. 2.
sp.l: No, Mr. Vicar:
The petty Chanon Hugh.

sp.speaker: Gro. 1.
sp.l: And Caſt-by Clay:
There they are all.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Then cry a Hall, a Hall!
'Tis merry in Tot tenham Hall, when beards wag all.
Come Father Rozin with your Fidle now,
• ond muſicke. And two tall-toters; Flouriſh to the Maſque.

div.head: ACT V. SCENE X.
Lady Preamble before her. Tub. Turfe. D. Turfe. Pol-marten, Awdrey. Puppy. Wiſpe. Hugh. Clay. All take their Seats. Hilts waits on the by.

sp.speaker: Lad.
sp.l: Neighbours, all welcome: Now doth Totten-Hall
Shew like a Court: and hence ſhall firſt be call'd ſo.
Your witty ſhort confeſſion Mr. Vicar,
Within hath beene the Prologue, and hath open'd
Much to my ſonnes device, his Tale of a Tub.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: Let my Maſque ſhew it ſelfe: And In-and-In,
The Architect, appeare; I heare the whiſtle. Hil. Peace.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: Thus riſe I firſt, in my light linnen breeches, Medlay appeares above the Curtain.
To run the meaning over in ſhort ſpeeches.
Here is a Tub; A Tub of Totten-Court:
An ancient Tub, hath call'd you to this ſport:
His Father was a Knight, the rich Sir Peeter;
Who got his wealth by a Tub, and by Salt-Peeter:
And left all to his Lady Tub; the mother
Of this bold Squire Tub, and to no other.
Now of this Tub, and's deeds, not done in ale,He drawes the Curtain, and diſcovers the top of the Tub. Hil. Ha' Peace. Loud Muſick.
Obſerve, and you ſhall ſee the very Tale.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: Here Chanon Hugh, firſt brings to Totten-Hall
The high Conſtables councell, tels the Squire all;
Which, though diſcover'd (give the Divell his due:)
The wiſe of Finsbury doe ſtill purſue.
Then with the Juſtice, doth he counterplot,
And his Clarke Metaphore, to cut that knot:
Whilſt Lady Tub, in her ſad velvet Gowne,
Miſſing her ſonne, doth ſeeke him up and downe.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: With her Pol-marten bare before her.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.p: Yes, I have expreſt it here in figure, and Miſtris Wiſpe her woman, holding up her traine.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: I'the next page, report your ſecond ſtraine.

div.head: The ſecond Motion. Hil. Ha' Peace. Loud Muſick.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: Here the high Conſtable, and Sages walke
To Church, the Dame, the Daughter, Bride-maids talke,
Of wedding buſines; till a fellow in comes,
Relates the robbery of one Captaine Thum's:
Chargeth the Bride-groome with it: Troubles all,
And gets the Bride; who in the hands doth fall
Of the bold Squire, but thence ſoone is tane
By the ſly Juſtice, and his Clarke profane
In ſhape of Purſuyvant; which he not long
Holds, but betrayes all with his trembling tongue:
As truth will breake out, and ſhew, &c.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: O thou haſt made him kneele there in a corner,
I ſee now: there is ſimple honour for you Hilts!

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: Did I not make him to confeſſe all to you?

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: True; In-and-In hath done you right, you ſee.
Thy third I pray thee, witty In-and-In.

sp.speaker: Cle.
sp.l: The Squire commends 'un. He doth like all well.

sp.speaker: Pan.
sp.l: Hee cannot chooſe. This is geare made to ſell.

div.head: Hil. Ha' peace. Loud muſick The third Motion.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: The carefull Conſtable, here drooping comes,
In his deluded ſearch, of Captaine Thum's.
Puppy brings word, his Daughter's run away.
With the tall Serving-man. He frights Groome Clay,
Out of his wits. Returneth then the Squire,
Mocks all their paines, and gives Fame out a Lyar:
For falſely charging Clay, when 'twas the plot,
Of ſubtile Bramble, who had Awdrey got,
Into his hand, by this winding device.
The Father makes a reskue in a trice:
And with his Daughter, like Saint George on foot,
Comes home triumphing, to his deare Hart root.
And tell's the Lady Tub, whom he meets there,
Of her ſonnes courteſies, the Batchelor.
Whoſe words had made 'hem fall the Huy and Cry.
When Captaine Thum's comming to aske him, why
He had ſo done? He cannot yeeld him cauſe:
But ſo he runs his neck into the Lawes.

div.head: Hil. Ha' peace. Loud Muſick. The fourth Motion.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: The Lawes, who have a nooſe to crack his neck,
As Juſtice Bramble tels him, who doth peck
A hundreth pound out of his purſe, that comes
Like his teeth from him, unto Captaine Thum's.
Thum's is the Vicar in a falſe diſguiſe:
And employes Metaphore, to fetch this prize.
Who tels the ſecret unto Basket-Hilts,
For feare of beating. This the Squire quilts
Within his Cap; and bids him but purloine
The wench for him: they two ſhall ſhare the coine.
Which the ſage Lady in her 'foreſaid Gowne
Breaks off, returning unto Kentiſh-Towne,
To ſeeke her Wiſpe; taking the Squire along,
Who finds Clay Iohn, as hidden in ſtraw throng.

sp.speaker: Hil.
sp.l: O, how am I beholden to the Inventer,
That would not, on record againſt me enter!
My ſlackneſſe here, to enter in the barne,
Well In-and-In, I ſee thou canſt diſcerne!

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l: On with your laſt, and come to a Concluſion.

div.head: The fift Motion. Hil. Ha' peace. Loud Muſicke.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: The laſt is knowne, and needs but ſmall infuſion
Into your memories, by leaving in
Theſe Figures as you ſit. I, In-and-In,
Preſent you with the ſhow: Firſt of a Lady
Tub, and her ſonne, of whom this Maſque here, made I.
Then Bride-groome Pol, and Miſtris Pol the Bride:
With the ſub-couple, who ſit them beſide.

sp.speaker: Tub.
sp.l:
That onely verſe, I alter'd for the better, 〈 in non-Latin alphabet 〉
gratid.

sp.speaker: Med.
sp.l: Then Juſtice Bramble, with Sir Hugh the Chanon:
And the Bride's Parents, which I will not ſtan'on,
Or the loſt Clay, with the recovered Giles:
Who thus unto his Maſter, him 'conciles,
On the Squires word, to pay old Turfe his Club,
And ſo doth end our Tale, here, of a Tub.
